text.title: RAG Canary Rollout Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every RAG-feature canary rollout must satisfy. These prevent the dominant failure: quality regressions that latency and error-rate canaries do not see.

rule.statement: The canary curve is fixed: 1% (24h hold) → 5% (24h) → 25% (48h) → 100%. No step is skipped; each hold must elapse before promotion. Holds can be extended if quality data is inconclusive, but never shortened.
rule.rationale: A 1% step catches catastrophic regressions; the 5% step catches subtle ones that 1% sample missed; the 25% step exercises real traffic volume. Skipping steps removes a layer of protection; shortening holds prevents enough quality samples from accumulating to compute a reliable score.

example.title: Canary curve in plan
example.code: rollout_plan: feature_id: support-bot-rag-v3 steps: - percent: 1 ; hold_hours: 24 ; min_samples: 200 - percent: 5 ; hold_hours: 24 ; min_samples: 1000 - percent: 25 ; hold_hours: 48 ; min_samples: 5000 - percent: 100 ; hold_hours: 168 ; soak_window: true

rule.statement: Before each step promotion, the golden eval suite runs against the canary configuration. The eval must show: (a) no regression on the primary quality metric vs the live baseline, (b) no regression > 5% on any secondary metric, (c) latency p95 within +20% of baseline. Step promotion is blocked until golden eval passes.
rule.rationale: Golden eval catches regressions that have not yet manifested in the small canary sample. Combining it with online quality monitoring is defense-in-depth: golden eval is fast and complete; online quality is slow but exercises real distributions.

rule.statement: A sample (typically 5-10% of canary traffic, bounded at 200-1000 samples per hour) is scored online for answer quality using a rubric: groundedness (claims supported by retrieved passages), relevance (answer addresses the question), completeness (covers the user's intent), no-hallucination (no fabricated facts). The judge is either an LLM-as-judge or a human review queue; rubric weights are documented.

rationale.source: LLM-as-judge research; production RAG quality measurement

rule.rationale: Latency and error rate are silent on RAG quality. A sampled rubric-based score is the only signal that catches "the answer was returned with status 200 but it was wrong". 5-10% sampling balances cost against statistical reliability.

rule.statement: Auto-rollback fires when: (a) online quality score drops > 10% below baseline for 5 consecutive minutes, OR (b) hallucination rate exceeds 2% of sampled answers, OR (c) latency p95 exceeds +50% of baseline, OR (d) error rate exceeds 3x baseline. Each criterion is pre-committed in rollout-plan.yaml; the rollback fires automatically without human approval.
rule.rationale: Manual rollback during a quality incident loses 10-30 minutes to "wait, let me check the dashboard". Auto-rollback restores the baseline immediately; human review of the incident comes after the user impact is stopped. Pre-committed criteria prevent the post-hoc rationalisation pattern.

rule.statement: Rollback completes in < 60 seconds from trigger to traffic restored to the previous version. Gateway routes shift atomically; no rebuild, no deploy, no cache-bust. Rollback execution is rehearsed quarterly via game-day exercise. Receipts are recorded automatically with trigger reason, executed_at, completed_within_seconds.

rationale.source: MTTR research; chaos-engineering practice for LLM rollback

rule.rationale: A rollback that takes 5 minutes might as well not exist when customer impact is accumulating per second. The 60-second contract forces the gateway and the deployment system to support atomic flips. Game-day exercises catch the rot that turns a working rollback into a broken one over months.

list.item: groundedness 0-3 — claims supported by retrieved passages
no-hallucination 0-3 — no fabricated facts or sources
composite_score = mean of the four; floor at 2.0/3.0 by default
